description: Multi-memory: architecture (Kafka event bus) + convention (every consumer must be idempotent) from one conversation

conversation.title: Standing up the platform-wide event bus

turns.text: Cross-service async work is currently a pile of bespoke webhooks. We need a platform-level event bus. The candidates are Kafka (Confluent Cloud), AWS EventBridge, and NATS JetStream. We want at-least-once delivery, ordering per partition key, replay, and a path to ~50K events/sec within 18 months. Pick one and tell me how to wire it up.

For your throughput target + ordering + replay requirement, Kafka is the safe pick. EventBridge is great for low-volume integration patterns but breaks down at 50K/s; NATS JetStream is excellent technically but the ops experience for a small team is heavier than managed Kafka. Architectural shape: one Kafka cluster (managed via Confluent Cloud to skip running ZooKeeper/KRaft yourselves), topics per *event family* not per service, partition key = the natural sharding key for that event family (e.g., `user_id` for user events). Producers go through a small in-process library that handles schema-registry registration; consumers run as separate worker processes per service, scaled independently of web.

Going with that. Architecture: Confluent Cloud Kafka as the platform-wide async event bus, topics-per-event-family, partition key = natural sharding key. Producer/consumer wrapper library lives in `platform-events` and handles Avro schema registration. Consumers are separate worker processes per service. And let's make it a convention: every event consumer must be idempotent — the same event applied twice produces the same end state. We rely on idempotency for replay and for at-least-once retries; non-idempotent consumers are not allowed to merge.

Two solid calls. The idempotency convention pairs nicely with two implementation rules you'll converge on naturally: (1) every event carries an `event_id` UUID, and consumers maintain a small `processed_events` table keyed on that id with a TTL, (2) for events that touch external APIs, the consumer library auto-attaches the same `event_id` as the API request idempotency key so duplicate replays don't create duplicate side-effects.

expected.note: Architecture: Confluent Cloud Kafka as the platform-wide event bus.
Forward-looking rule: every event consumer must be idempotent. 'idempoten' stem matches idempotent/idempotency.
